revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23986660476|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[ja:神功皇后神社 (豊北町)]] {{Infobox religious building | Name = Empress Jingū Shrine | image = File:Zinnkoukougouzinzta toyokitatyou honden P1180459.JPG | Religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Location = Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimonoseki City, Toyokita Town, Kanda Kamisen 1700 | Deity = Empress Jingū
Emperor Chūai
Emperor Ōjin
Sumiyoshi Sanjin
Kotoshironushi-no-Kami
Hiruko-no-Kami
Local National Martyrs | Established = First Year of Shōō (1288) July 10 | Festival = November 8 | Rituals = Hama-den Festival: A ritual held every seven years to pacify the spirits from the Mongol invasions and pray for the eradication of epidemics and peace. | location = {{ill|Shimonoseki|qid=Q203308|1=Shimonoseki}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.294268|N|130.879733|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} }} '''Empress Jingū Shrine''' (神功皇后神社, Jingū Kōgō Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in [[Toyokita Town]], [[Shimonoseki City]], {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|qid=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}, Japan. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Empress Jingū, Emperor Chūai, Emperor Ōjin, Sumiyoshi Sanjin, Kotoshironushi-no-Kami, Hiruko-no-Kami, and local national martyrs. == Overview == During the [[Kōan Era]] (1278 - ), when the combined Mongol and Goryeo forces invaded Doi-no-hama, the samurai dispatched under the orders of {{ill|Hōjō Tokimune|en|Hōjō Tokimune|qid=Q430964|lt=Hōjō Tokimune|1=Hōjō Tokimune}} were concerned about the difficulty of repelling the Mongol forces. They offered prayers to Empress Jingū, recalling her conquest of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|qid=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}. When divine intervention manifested, the Mongol forces were successfully repelled. In gratitude, in the first year of {{ill|Shōō|en|Shōō|qid=Q1156223|lt=Shōō|1=Shōō}} (1288), construction of a shrine began on January 29 and was completed on July 10. The shrine houses cultural treasures, including a pair of wooden guardian lion-dogs (''komainu'') from the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} and a Mongol helmet, which can be viewed in the on-site ''Hama-den Festival Exhibition Hall'' (Reikō Art Museum). [[File:Hamadonosai bizyutukanP1180454.JPG|thumb|Hama-den Festival Exhibition Hall]] [[File:Simonoseki moukotuka P1180440.JPG|thumb|Cultural treasure: Mongol halberd]] == History == * Kōan Era - The Mongol invasions of Japan; the Mongol-Goryeo allied forces attack [[Doi-no-hama]]. * First year of {{ill|Shōō|en|Shōō|qid=Q1156223|lt=Shōō|1=Shōō}} (1288) - Construction of the shrine begins on January 29. * July 10, 1288 - Shrine construction is completed. * Third year of {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|qid=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei|1=Kan'ei}} (1626) - Shrine buildings are reconstructed. * November 24, 1976 - The Hama-den Festival is designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Yamaguchi Prefecture. * March 1982 - Current worship hall is built. * June 2008 - Hama-den Festival Exhibition Hall (Reikō Art Museum) opens. == Major Festivals == The ''Hama-den Festival'' is held every seven years to pacify the spirits from the Mongol invasions and pray for the eradication of epidemics and peace. A procession led by the ''Hanamiko'' (flower maiden), ''Shouga'' (warrior leader), and ''Otona'' (elders) parades elegantly on horseback to Doi-no-hama. On November 24, 1976, it was designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Yamaguchi Prefecture. * January 1 - New Year's Festival * December 31 - New Year's Eve Festival == Nearby Sites == * [[Doi-no-hama Ruins]] * [http://www.doigahama.jp/ Doi-no-hama Archaeological Museum] == References == * On-site historical marker (stone inscription, 1986) == See Also == * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|en|Mongol invasions of Japan|qid=Q208156|lt=Mongol invasions of Japan|1=Mongol invasions of Japan}} * [[Shinjō Shrine]] * [[Komoda-hama Shrine]] * [[Mongol Invasion Museum]] * [[Doi-no-hama]] == External Links == * [http://nobyama.com/jinguukougou.html Empress Jingū Shrine] * {{Wayback|url=http://blogs.yahoo.co.jp/to_genkou/54413569.html |title=Mongol Halberd of Empress Jingū Shrine |date=20191101000000}} * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HDKYhB1-UFI Hama-den Festival 2011] * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pt76hcqwU6E Hama-den Festival 2004] {{commonscat|}} {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|神功皇后神社 (豊北町)|version=87752963|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:神功皇后神社 (豊北町)]] {{Commons category|Jingū-kōgō-jinja (Shimonoseki)}} {{wikidata link|Q17993865}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shimonoseki]] [[Category:History of Shimonoseki]] [[Category:Mongol invasions of Japan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
